title: 53rd Academy Awards

infobox.value: [Dorothy Chandler Pavilion](/wiki/Dorothy_Chandler_Pavilion "Dorothy Chandler Pavilion") [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California, U.S.
*[The Elephant Man](/wiki/The_Elephant_Man_(1980_film) "The Elephant Man (1980 film)")* and *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (8)

ceremony, organized by the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences") (AMPAS), honored [films released in 1980](/wiki/1980_in_film "1980 in film") and took place on March 31, 1981, at the [Dorothy Chandler Pavilion](/wiki/Dorothy_Chandler_Pavilion "Dorothy Chandler Pavilion") in Los Angeles, beginning at 7:00 p.m. [PST](/wiki/Pacific_Time_Zone "Pacific Time Zone") / 10:00 p.m. [EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone"). The ceremony was scheduled to take place originally on the previous day but was postponed due to the [attempted assassination](/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Ronald_Reagan "Attempted assassination of Ronald Reagan") of [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan").[[2]](#cite_note-2) During the ceremony, AMPAS presented [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") (commonly referred to as Oscars) in 20 categories. The ceremony, televised in the United States by [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), was produced by [Norman Jewison](/wiki/Norman_Jewison "Norman Jewison") and directed by [Marty Pasetta](/wiki/Marty_Pasetta "Marty Pasetta").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Comedian and talk show host [Johnny Carson](/wiki/Johnny_Carson "Johnny Carson") hosted the show for the third consecutive time. Two weeks earlier, in a ceremony held at [The Beverly Hilton](/wiki/The_Beverly_Hilton "The Beverly Hilton") in [Beverly Hills, California](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_California "Beverly Hills, California"), on March 15, the [Academy Scientific and Technical Awards](/wiki/Academy_Scientific_and_Technical_Awards "Academy Scientific and Technical Awards") were presented by hosts [Ed Asner](/wiki/Ed_Asner "Ed Asner") and [Fay Kanin](/wiki/Fay_Kanin "Fay Kanin").[[4]](#cite_note-4)
and indicated with a double dagger (‡).[[11]](#cite_note-Oscars1981-11)

The **53rd Academy Awards** ceremony, organized by the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences") (AMPAS), honored [films released in 1980](/wiki/1980_in_film "1980 in film") and took place on March 31, 1981, at the [Dorothy Chandler Pavilion](/wiki/Dorothy_Chandler_Pavilion "Dorothy Chandler Pavilion") in Los Angeles, beginning at 7:00 p.m. [PST](/wiki/Pacific_Time_Zone "Pacific Time Zone") / 10:00 p.m. [EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone"). The ceremony was scheduled to take place originally on the previous day but was postponed due to the [attempted assassination](/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Ronald_Reagan "Attempted assassination of Ronald Reagan") of [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan").[[2]](#cite_note-2) During the ceremony, AMPAS presented [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") (commonly referred to as Oscars) in 20 categories. The ceremony, televised in the United States by [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), was produced by [Norman Jewison](/wiki/Norman_Jewison "Norman Jewison") and directed by [Marty Pasetta](/wiki/Marty_Pasetta "Marty Pasetta").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Comedian and talk show host [Johnny Carson](/wiki/Johnny_Carson "Johnny Carson") hosted the show for the third consecutive time. Two weeks earlier, in a ceremony held at [The Beverly Hilton](/wiki/The_Beverly_Hilton "The Beverly Hilton") in [Beverly Hills, California](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_California "Beverly Hills, California"), on March 15, the [Academy Scientific and Technical Awards](/wiki/Academy_Scientific_and_Technical_Awards "Academy Scientific and Technical Awards") were presented by hosts [Ed Asner](/wiki/Ed_Asner "Ed Asner") and [Fay Kanin](/wiki/Fay_Kanin "Fay Kanin").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

*[Ordinary People](/wiki/Ordinary_People "Ordinary People")* won four awards, including [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Other winners included *[Tess](/wiki/Tess_(1979_film) "Tess (1979 film)")* with three awards, *[The Empire Strikes Back](/wiki/The_Empire_Strikes_Back "The Empire Strikes Back")*, *[Fame](/wiki/Fame_(1980_film) "Fame (1980 film)")*, *[Melvin and Howard](/wiki/Melvin_and_Howard "Melvin and Howard")*, and *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* with two, and *[Coal Miner's Daughter](/wiki/Coal_Miner%27s_Daughter_(film) "Coal Miner's Daughter (film)")*, *[The Dollar Bottom](/wiki/The_Dollar_Bottom "The Dollar Bottom")*, *[The Fly](/wiki/The_Fly_(1980_film) "The Fly (1980 film)")*, *[From Mao to Mozart: Isaac Stern in China](/wiki/From_Mao_to_Mozart:_Isaac_Stern_in_China "From Mao to Mozart: Isaac Stern in China")*, *[Karl Hess: Toward Liberty](/wiki/Karl_Hess:_Toward_Liberty "Karl Hess: Toward Liberty")*, and *[Moscow Does Not Believe in Tears](/wiki/Moscow_Does_Not_Believe_in_Tears "Moscow Does Not Believe in Tears")* with one. The telecast garnered 39.9 million viewers in the United States.

## Winners and nominees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=53rd_Academy_Awards&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Winners and nominees")]

The nominees for the 53rd Academy Awards were announced on February 17, 1981, by Academy president [Fay Kanin](/wiki/Fay_Kanin "Fay Kanin") and actor [William Devane](/wiki/William_Devane "William Devane").[[6]](#cite_note-6) *[The Elephant Man](/wiki/The_Elephant_Man_(1980_film) "The Elephant Man (1980 film)")* and *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* tied for the most nominations with eight each.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The winners were announced at the awards ceremony on March 31. Best Director winner [Robert Redford](/wiki/Robert_Redford "Robert Redford") became the third individual to win this category for his directing debut and the first actor to achieve this feat.[[8]](#cite_note-8) At age 20, Best Supporting Actor winner [Timothy Hutton](/wiki/Timothy_Hutton "Timothy Hutton") was the youngest male acting winner in Oscar history.[[9]](#cite_note-9) *[Fame](/wiki/Fame_(1980_film) "Fame (1980 film)")* became the first film to earn two nominations for Best Original Song.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Ceremony information

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=53rd_Academy_Awards&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ceremony information")]

[![Johnny Carson in 1970](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Johnny_Carson_1970.JPG/250px-Johnny_Carson_1970.JPG)](/wiki/File:Johnny_Carson_1970.JPG)

[Johnny Carson](/wiki/Johnny_Carson "Johnny Carson") hosted the 53rd Academy Awards.

In December 1980, the Academy hired film producer [Norman Jewison](/wiki/Norman_Jewison "Norman Jewison") to produce the telecast for the first time. "I am delighted that the Academy will have the benefit of Norman Jewison's insight and creativity," said AMPAS President [Fay Kanin](/wiki/Fay_Kanin "Fay Kanin") in a press release announcing the selection. "He has always been able to bring a fresh approach into his work."[[16]](#cite_note-17) That same month, it was announced that comedian and *[The Tonight Show](/wiki/The_Tonight_Show_Starring_Johnny_Carson "The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson")* host [Johnny Carson](/wiki/Johnny_Carson "Johnny Carson") would preside over emceeing duties for the 1981 ceremony. Jewison explained the decision to hire Carson, saying that the host was "an entertainment institution whose spontaneous wit and charm would add a great deal to the quality of the show."[[17]](#cite_note-18)

Originally, the gala was scheduled to take place on March 30. However, due to the [attempted assassination](/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Ronald_Reagan "Attempted assassination of Ronald Reagan") on US president [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") which occurred earlier that day, Kanin, Jewison, and executives from broadcaster [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") announced that the festivities would be postponed to the following day.[[18]](#cite_note-19) It marked the first time since the [40th ceremony](/wiki/40th_Academy_Awards "40th Academy Awards") held in 1968 that the ceremony was postponed from its original date.[[19]](#cite_note-20) Furthermore, producers debated whether or not to televise a pre-recorded speech from Reagan, who was a former actor, saluting the nominees and the Academy. The segment, which was filmed nearly four weeks prior to the show, was eventually broadcast, with host Carson giving a preface explaining the decision to postpone the event.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### *The Fly* acceptance speech

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=53rd_Academy_Awards&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Fly acceptance speech")]

During the presentation of the award for Best Animated Short Film to *[The Fly](/wiki/The_Fly_(1980_film) "The Fly (1980 film)")*, presenters [Alan Arkin](/wiki/Alan_Arkin "Alan Arkin") and [Margot Kidder](/wiki/Margot_Kidder "Margot Kidder") announced that the film's director, [Ferenc Rofusz](/wiki/Ferenc_Rofusz "Ferenc Rofusz") was unable to attend the ceremony. Just as they announced the Academy would accept the award on his behalf, an unnamed man later identified as Hungarofilm general manager Istvan Dosai came up on stage and accepted the award in lieu of the absent filmmaker.[[21]](#cite_note-Ferenc-22) Marble Arch Films publicist Regina Gruss, who was in charge of hosting the Hungarian delegation at the Oscars, said that Rofusz contacted Dosai to accept the award on his behalf, but Academy officials asked him not to come up onstage unless his name was announced.[[22]](#cite_note-23) After speaking to reporters and posing for pictures backstage, he never returned to his seat and left the ceremony immediately.[[21]](#cite_note-Ferenc-22) According to Academy security chief Jerry Moon, AMPAS contacted the [LAPD](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department") to issue a search warrant for Dosai for theft. However, Academy spokesperson Art Sarno denied that the organization had contacted the police and said that Dosai returned the statuette during a post-awards banquet.[[23]](#cite_note-Dosai-24)

### Critical reviews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=53rd_Academy_Awards&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Critical reviews")]

Some media outlets received the broadcast critically. Television columnist [Tom Shales](/wiki/Tom_Shales "Tom Shales") of *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* commented, "Everything seemed an anticlimax to the Reagan opening, and the tragic events in Washington a day earlier did put a shadow of gloom over an affair that had promised to be grim enough anyway – since all but one of the year's Best Picture nominees were somber, austere films, and nothing to shout about."[[24]](#cite_note-25) *[The Salt Lake Tribune](/wiki/The_Salt_Lake_Tribune "The Salt Lake Tribune")* television critic [Harold Schindler](/wiki/Harold_Schindler "Harold Schindler") wrote, "On the whole, the 53rd annual Academy Awards telecast Tuesday was overly long, expectedly dull and surprisingly lacking in those highlights which make Oscar night conversation."[[25]](#cite_note-26) Bill Mandel of the *[San Francisco Examiner](/wiki/San_Francisco_Examiner "San Francisco Examiner")* quipped, "After the real and completely unscripted emotional explosions of Monday, all the manipulated thrills of the movie industry seemed like the efforts of those bullfight clowns who distract the bull when the matador is injured."[[26]](#cite_note-27)

Other media outlets received the broadcast more positively. *[Tampa Bay Times](/wiki/St._Petersberg_Times "St. Petersberg Times")* film critic Robert Alan Ross remarked, "The one-day delay turned out well. President Reagan's taped greeting – combined with emcee Johnny Carson's assurance that the First Couple were comfortably watching – instilled a happier mood than might otherwise have prevailed."[[27]](#cite_note-28) Jerry Buck of the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") quipped, "Producer Norman Jewison effectively chose to make the night a homage to motion pictures' past, making wide use of many cherished film clips that tugged at the heart and memory."[[28]](#cite_note-29) *[The Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")* columnist Bruce McCabe wrote, "Given the trauma of the past few days, the Academy Awards show conducted itself rather well. No one really disgraced himself. There were no political speeches."[[29]](#cite_note-30)

### Ratings and reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=53rd_Academy_Awards&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ratings and reception")]

The American telecast on ABC drew in an average of 39.9 million people over the length of the entire ceremony, which was a 19% decrease from the [previous year's ceremony](/wiki/52nd_Academy_Awards "52nd Academy Awards").[[30]](#cite_note-31) An estimated 75 million total viewers watched all or part of the awards.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Moreover, the show drew lower [Nielsen ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings") compared to the previous ceremony, with 31% of households watching over a 58% share.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Nevertheless, the ceremony presentation won an award for Outstanding Art Direction for a Variety Program ([Roy Christopher](/wiki/Roy_Christopher "Roy Christopher")) at the [33rd Primetime Emmys](/wiki/33rd_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "33rd Primetime Emmy Awards") in September 1981.[[33]](#cite_note-34)
